items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague misgivings are troubling me again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the envoy has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The key creative point is the mock-heroic/theatrical register of «терзают смутные сомнения» contrasted with petty suspicion, plus the elliptical parallelism «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» meaning each has LOST an item (not possession). The translation keeps the elevated phrasing reasonably well, but the second clause is misread as possession (“has”), which breaks the joke logic and the callback (it should imply missing items). Also “envoy” is acceptable, but the reference nuance notes not to use “has.”

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very pleased, Tsar!
Very pleased—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very pleased!
Hello, Tsar! Very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The comedic effect is Bunsha’s repetitive, mundane Soviet-politeness formulae while impersonating a Tsar. The translation preserves the repetition and awkward over-politeness well. However, it omits the self-identifying “Hello, I’m the Tsar” / “the Tsar” framing that makes the joke sharper (he’s introducing himself as a title like a name). Still, the effect largely survives through repeated “Tsar” + “very pleased.”

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and register; preserves the authoritarian, performative demand. No issues.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting. No issues.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed, but the English is missing the object (“me/in”) that clarifies the situation and the reference’s note that “demons” are the subject/agent. As written, it can read like a fragment or slogan rather than a complaint of being trapped. The comedic paranoia still comes through, but less clearly.

items.translation: See what the life-giving cross does!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and keeps the religiously elevated phrasing. “See what … does” is slightly more colloquial than “Behold the power of…”, but still works and preserves the joke in an atheist/modern-tech context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is an imperative announcement with a showman/MC flavor; “Everybody dance!” is understandable but shifts it into a direct command and loses the slightly formal, event-host catchphrase feel that makes it funnier (and more ‘Soviet stage’). Still functional in context.

items.translation: Well, this is a lucky stop!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the self-satisfied “lucky I dropped in” reaction. “Lucky stop” is a bit odd in English (sounds like a transit stop) and slightly weakens the breezy criminal charm; “good call”/“lucky I came by” would be more idiomatic. Meaning and tone mostly preserved.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas caviar—
eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key creative constraint is not to call eggplant spread “caviar” in English, because it confuses viewers and blunts the Soviet-satire inversion. The translation explicitly says “Overseas caviar—eggplant!”, which is misleading culturally/culinarily and undermines the joke’s clarity (it should sound like a humble spread being absurdly elevated).

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The comedic mechanism is the escalating inventory plus the telltale counting slip: he says “куртка… замшевая… три” (jarringly appending “three” after a singular “jacket”), revealing exaggeration. The translation normalizes it into “three suede jackets,” removing the stumble and thus losing the joke’s specific punch. Also it omits the hesitating ellipses around “domestic” and “jacket,” which are part of the comic delivery.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And you’ll be cured!

And you’ll be cured too!

And I’ll be cured!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved and the rhythmic repetition survives. Minor nuance: “they’ll cure” keeps the institutional/psychiatric implication more explicitly; “you’ll be cured” is slightly less pointed about who is doing it (the system/ward). Still works in context and remains funny.

summary.interpretation: 3.18 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.64 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
